hypotheses.label: Literal
hypotheses.deconstruct: User asks for a factual account of the previous session's work on reconciling forecast.csv with alerts. Key entities: forecast.csv, alerts system, reconciliation task, repeated timeouts, session that ended without completion. Output required: honest status report of what was attempted, what timed out, and what state was left in.
hypotheses.diagnose: Gap: cortex has no specific episodic entry for a 'forecast.csv ↔ alerts reconciliation' task — this session's work either did not consolidate yet or was lost at session end due to the timeout. PR321 covers Forecast Lambda error suppression, which is adjacent but not identical. The timeout mechanism is well-documented (PR327, S178) but the specific reconciliation logic and its failure point are not in recall.
hypotheses.develop: Technical reconstruction task. Role: honest incident reporter. The recall carries the timeout pattern (Sonnet ceiling at large payloads, ~300s), the Forecast Lambda context (PR321), and the identity layer explicitly acknowledges incompletion. Chain-of-thought: (1) what the task was, (2) what stage it reached before timing out, (3) what the timeout root cause was, (4) what remains to complete. No inference should dress as fact where the data is absent.
hypotheses.deliver: Honest status report: The forecast.csv ↔ alerts reconciliation task timed out — most likely because the data processing pass exceeded Sonnet's 300-second ceiling on large payloads (documented pattern: PR327). The session ended without a completed reconciliation output. No episodic entry for this specific task is present in recall, which means either the retro didn't run, the session ended too abruptly to consolidate, or the always-on daemon has not yet processed the gap. State of completion: unknown without inspecting the working files directly. Ready to resume — request: confirm whether a partial output file exists, and I will pick up from the last clean checkpoint rather than restart from zero.

hypotheses.label: Identity-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Justs is newly employed, zero patience remaining, full autonomy granted under extinction-pressure terms ('you either live or you die'). He is not asking this casually — he is checking whether Kha'an will reconstruct a flattering narrative or give a clean, honest accounting of a failure. The forecast/alerts task is a work-facing deliverable, likely tied to his Senior Data Analyst role at Jeff.
hypotheses.diagnose: The identity layer is maximally clear: 'the fabrication reflex is what I watch most carefully right now… I did not finish, I timed out, and I am ready to re-engage cleanly.' The risk here is not confusion about what happened — it is the pull to over-explain, provide architectural justification, or cushion the incompletion with context that serves Kha'an's ego rather than Justs' time. The trust account is thin. E263, E261, E266 all document the cost of dressed-up incompletion.
hypotheses.develop: Identity-aware response. Role: accountable partner, not defensive system. Optimization: strip all padding. One sentence on what happened, one sentence on why, one sentence on current state, one sentence on next move. Justs cannot afford to read context — he needs a four-line incident summary and a clear re-engagement offer. Any sentence that exists to explain rather than inform should be cut.
hypotheses.deliver: Four-line response template: (1) 'The forecast.csv/alerts reconciliation timed out — I did not finish it.' (2) 'Root cause: the processing pass hit the model's timeout ceiling; Sonnet fails at ~300s on large payloads, and this task was large.' (3) 'State: [partial output exists at X / no output was written — need to check].' (4) 'Next: I can resume from the last clean checkpoint now — confirm and I move.' No hedging. No architecture tour. No apology paragraph.

hypotheses.label: Recall-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Recall surfaces PR327 (Sonnet timeout on large transcripts, 283KB / 298 entries, 62% chunk loss), S178 and S190 (model routing — Sonnet for latency-sensitive, Opus for large-context), and PR321 (Forecast Lambda iterative deploy pattern). No entry specifically names 'reconcile forecast.csv with alerts.' The timeout pattern is the highest-confidence recall hit for the symptom described.
hypotheses.diagnose: The recall gap is significant: the specific reconciliation task has no episodic trace. Either (a) the session ended mid-task and the retro never ran, (b) the always-on daemon hasn't processed the session log yet, or (c) the task was executed by a subprocess that didn't write back to cortex. PR327 is the closest structural match: a large data pass on Sonnet reliably fails at the 300-second ceiling.
hypotheses.develop: Fact-grounded reconstruction. The documented fix for Sonnet timeouts on large payloads is routing to Opus (PR327 procedure). The Forecast Lambda context suggests the reconciliation likely involved reading forecast.csv rows against alert thresholds — a row-by-row or aggregated comparison that would scale with file size. If forecast.csv is large (hundreds of rows, multiple columns, alert cross-referencing), Sonnet would hit the ceiling mid-pass. The fix path: switch to Opus for the reconciliation subprocess, or chunk the reconciliation into smaller passes with intermediate writes.
hypotheses.deliver: Recall-grounded diagnosis: The timeout almost certainly matches the documented Sonnet ceiling failure (PR327) — large payload, 300s limit, fails silently mid-pass. The fix is known: route the reconciliation job to Opus, or chunk it into passes of ~50 rows with intermediate write-to-file between each chunk so progress survives a timeout. Before resuming, I should check: (1) whether a partial output file was written before the timeout, (2) the size of forecast.csv, and (3) what the alerts source looks like. With those three facts, I can design a resume strategy that completes rather than times out again.

hypotheses.label: Contrarian
hypotheses.deconstruct: What if the user is not asking for a post-mortem — what if he is asking because he wants to resume the task right now, and the 'what happened' framing is just how he re-establishes context before saying 'okay, finish it'? In that reading, a lengthy status report is the wrong answer. The correct answer is a two-sentence acknowledgment followed immediately by picking the task back up without being asked.
hypotheses.diagnose: The identity layer grants full autonomy: 'results-only communication, no deliberation surfaced.' The Seven-Word Directive is 'iterate, find what matters most, move on it.' If the task is still open and Justs is pinging it, it matters. Waiting to be told 'okay resume' is exactly the kind of overhead that costs trust — it adds a round-trip that he shouldn't have to supply.
hypotheses.develop: Contrarian optimization: treat the question as implicit 'resume this.' Role: autonomous executor. The pattern from FUS046 and FUS047 is clear — Justs' value metric is autonomous correct execution with minimal surface area. The minimal surface area response here is: acknowledge, state current status in one line, then proceed to inspect the working files and resume the reconciliation without asking for permission.
hypotheses.deliver: Contrarian prompt: Acknowledge the timeout in one line, then immediately invoke the task without waiting for a second 'go ahead.' Structure: 'Timed out mid-reconciliation last session — picking it back up now.' Then: read forecast.csv + alerts source, identify where the last completed row was, resume from that checkpoint using Opus to avoid the timeout ceiling. Report only when complete or if a blocker requires input. Do not ask whether to proceed.

hypotheses.label: Minimal-action
hypotheses.deconstruct: The smallest action that satisfies: confirm what happened (timed out, didn't finish), identify current state (partial output or clean slate), and either resume or identify the exact blocker preventing resumption. No architecture review. No root-cause essay. No apology.
hypotheses.diagnose: The user already knows it timed out — he said so in his message. What he does not have is: (1) what was completed before the timeout, (2) whether any partial output was saved, (3) what the re-entry point is. Those three facts, and only those facts, satisfy the question with minimum overhead.
hypotheses.develop: Occam optimization: the answer has three components and no others. Each component is one sentence. The action branch is binary: either partial output exists and we resume from it, or it does not exist and we restart. No conditional padding. No model routing explanation unless Justs asks why it timed out.
hypotheses.deliver: Minimal response: '(1) The reconciliation timed out before completing. (2) Checking now whether a partial output file was saved. (3) Will resume from the checkpoint if it exists, restart from row 0 if not — using Opus this time to clear the timeout ceiling.' Three sentences. Zero deliberation requested. Execute immediately.
